resumeData.title: AI Engineer & Full-Stack Developer
resumeData.subtitle: AI-Driven Solutions & Data Strategy
resumeData.summary: Hands-on developer specializing in data engineering, machine learning, and web development. Experience building high-frequency data aggregators for digital asset markets and robust data architectures for HealthTech platforms. Proficient across the full data lifecycle: scraping, ETL, deep learning model training (LSTM, GANs, YOLOv8), and deployment with Docker and FastAPI. Focused on clean code, automation, and interfaces that make complex data accessible.

engagements.client: Outlier AI, AI training and model evaluation platform
engagements.project: Supported AI model training by evaluating, annotating, and improving model responses across reasoning, factuality, and instruction-following tasks.
achievements: Evaluated AI responses for accuracy, clarity, safety, and instruction-following
Compared model outputs and identified hallucinations, reasoning errors, and inconsistencies
Provided structured annotations and feedback to improve LLM training data quality
Rewrote and refined responses based on project guidelines

engagements.project: Architected the complete data infrastructure for a nutritional tracking application (mobile and web).
achievements: Contributed to the development of a computer vision model for food segmentation and calorie estimation
Designed and deployed the relational database (SQL) schema from scratch, ensuring 3NF normalization for user logs, nutritional tables, and biometric data
Built ETL pipelines to scrape, clean, and ingest nutritional datasets
Established data cleaning standards to support the calorie recommendation engine

card.title: Order Analysis with Basic Python
card.shortDescription: A guided exercise in pure Python to build an e-commerce order report using lists, dictionaries, loops, and functions.

hero.label: Guided exercise
hero.title: Order Analysis with Basic Python
hero.subtitle: A guided exercise to practice pure Python before moving on to Pandas and data analysis.
hero.description: This project simulates a small e-commerce order dataset. Each order has a customer, state, status, and a list of items. Your goal is to complete Python functions that generate a final report in the terminal.

simulation.title: What does this project simulate?
simulation.intro: The exercise simulates a simple e-commerce order analysis. The structure is designed to feel like real data while using only basic Python.
fields: item list (product, category, unit price, quantity)

concepts.title: Concepts practiced
concepts.subtitle: The exercise combines lessons 01–09 into one analysis workflow.

dataStructure.title: Data structure
explanation: Each item in the list is a dictionary.
Each order has a key called itens.
itens is also a list of dictionaries.
This trains multi-level access, for example:

encouragement.title: You don't need to get everything right the first time
encouragement.body: If you get stuck on a function, that's okay. The goal of this project isn't to finish quickly—it's to practice reading the data, testing ideas, making mistakes, fixing them, and trying again. That process is part of learning to program.

pythonExercise.closingNote: What matters isn't a perfect solution on the first try. What matters is keeping at it until the logic starts to click.

implement.title: What you need to implement
implement.subtitle: Complete the functions marked with TODO in the .py file:

functions.name: calcular_total_pedido(pedido)
functions.desc: Loops through order items and sums totals.

functions.name: calcular_ticket_medio(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Total revenue divided by delivered order count.

functions.name: criar_ranking_clientes(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Ranks customers by spend on delivered orders.

functions.name: listar_pedidos_acima_de(lista_pedidos, valor_minimo=500)
functions.desc: Lists delivered orders above the minimum total.

functions.name: gerar_relatorio(lista_pedidos)
functions.desc: Builds and prints the full terminal report.

run.title: How to run
run.stepsIntro: Set up a local environment on your machine. Pick your system below and follow the terminal commands.
run.venvIntro: A venv (virtual environment) is an isolated folder just for this project. It keeps this exercise separate from other Python installs on your computer. Create it with python -m venv venv inside the project folder, then activate it before running the script.

linux.intro: Use the terminal (Bash). If python3 does not work, try python instead of python3 in the commands below.
linux.pythonNote: When active, your prompt usually shows (venv). To leave the environment later: deactivate.
steps: Create the folder analise-pedidos-python and cd into it.
Create the venv with python3 -m venv venv — this creates the venv/ subfolder.
With the venv active, run python analise_pedidos_guiado.py.

windows.intro: On Windows, PowerShell is the most common option today. If you prefer Command Prompt (cmd), use the alternative block below.
windows.powershellNote: If activation fails due to policy, run once in PowerShell: Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope CurrentUser RemoteSigned. Then try activating the venv again. To exit: deactivate.
steps: Create the folder analise-pedidos-python and cd into it.
Create the venv with python -m venv venv.
Activate the environment (PowerShell or cmd, per the block above).
With (venv) in the prompt, run python analise_pedidos_guiado.py.

commonSteps: Download or copy analise_pedidos_guiado.py into the project folder.
Open the folder in VS Code, PyCharm, or your editor.
Complete the functions marked with TODO.
Uncomment gerar_relatorio(pedidos) when you are ready to test.
Only then run the script with the virtual environment active.

output.subtitle: Format of the final terminal report (values may vary with the dataset):

github.title: Suggested GitHub delivery
github.intro: Create a simple repository and document your solution:

why.title: Why this exercise matters
why.body: Before using Pandas, it is important to understand how to manipulate data with pure Python. This project performs manually what libraries will simplify later.
why.quote: Before automating with Pandas, understand the logic behind the data.

cta.title: Ready to practice?
cta.body: Download the file, complete the functions, and publish your solution on GitHub.

pythonExercise.nextStep: The next study step will use Pandas to simplify this kind of analysis — this exercise bridges basic Python and data analysis.
